facts.text: Roger Federer, Rafael Nadal, and Novak Djokovic — the 'Big Three' — have combined to win over 60 Grand Slam singles titles.
Novak Djokovic holds the record for most men's Grand Slam singles titles with 24.
Wimbledon is the oldest tennis tournament in the world, first held in 1877.
The four Grand Slam tournaments are the Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon, and the US Open.
Serena Williams won 23 Grand Slam singles titles, the most in the Open Era for any player, male or female.
A tennis ball travels at speeds up to 163 mph on professional serves — the record set by Sam Groth in 2012.
The 2008 Wimbledon final between Roger Federer and Rafael Nadal is widely considered the greatest tennis match ever played.
Wimbledon's strict all-white dress code has been enforced since 1963.
A regulation tennis court is 78 feet long and 27 feet wide for singles (36 feet for doubles).
Margaret Court holds the all-time record for most Grand Slam singles titles with 24, though many were won before the Open Era.
Rafael Nadal has won the French Open a record 14 times, earning him the nickname 'The King of Clay.'
The US Open is the only Grand Slam that uses a tiebreak in the final set.
Steffi Graf achieved the 'Golden Slam' in 1988 — winning all four Grand Slams plus the Olympic gold medal in a single year.
The 'Hawk-Eye' electronic line-calling system was first used at a Grand Slam in 2006.
Billie Jean King defeated Bobby Riggs in the famous 'Battle of the Sexes' match in 1973, watched by 90 million viewers.
The longest tennis match in history was the 2010 Wimbledon first-round match between John Isner and Nicolas Mahut, lasting 11 hours and 5 minutes.
Venus and Serena Williams have faced each other in 9 Grand Slam finals, with Serena winning 7 of those matchups.
Roger Federer held the world number 1 ranking for a record 310 weeks.
The Australian Open is played on hard courts and is known as the 'Happy Slam' due to its friendly atmosphere.
Pete Sampras held the record for most men's Grand Slam titles (14) for over a decade before Federer surpassed it in 2009.
A tennis racket's 'sweet spot' is the area of the string bed that produces the most power and control.
Andre Agassi is one of only 5 players to complete the career Grand Slam (winning all four majors).
The Davis Cup is the premier international team event in men's tennis, first held in 1900.
Chris Evert won 18 Grand Slam singles titles and reached 34 Grand Slam finals during her career.
Novak Djokovic completed a non-calendar-year Grand Slam by winning four consecutive Slams from 2015-16.
The French Open is the only Grand Slam played on clay courts, which favors players with strong topspin and endurance.
Arthur Ashe was the first Black man to win the US Open (1968), Australian Open (1970), and Wimbledon (1975).
The 'let' serve — when the ball hits the net and lands in the correct service box — results in a replay of the serve.
Maria Sharapova won her first Wimbledon title at age 17 and went on to complete the career Grand Slam.
The tennis scoring system (15, 30, 40, game) likely originated from clock faces in medieval France.
Jimmy Connors played in a record 401 weeks in the ATP top 10, dominating men's tennis for over a decade.
The Laver Cup, inspired by golf's Ryder Cup, pits Team Europe against Team World in a team format.
Monica Seles was stabbed during a match in Hamburg in 1993 by a deranged Steffi Graf fan, derailing her career.
John McEnroe's famous outburst 'You cannot be serious!' at Wimbledon in 1981 became one of sports' most iconic phrases.
The Wilson Pro Staff, Babolat Pure Drive, and Head Speed are among the most popular professional tennis rackets.
Martina Navratilova won 18 Grand Slam singles titles and 31 Grand Slam doubles titles.
The ATP (Association of Tennis Professionals) rankings system was introduced in 1973.
Carlos Alcaraz won Wimbledon in 2023 at age 20, ending Novak Djokovic's 4-year reign on grass.
Iga Swiatek has dominated the women's tour, winning multiple French Open titles and holding the world number 1 ranking.
The 'Eastern grip,' 'Western grip,' and 'Continental grip' are the three basic ways to hold a tennis racket.
Ivan Lendl reached 19 Grand Slam finals and won 8 titles, later coaching Andy Murray to his Grand Slam breakthroughs.
Coco Gauff became the youngest player to qualify for Wimbledon's main draw since 1991 when she was just 15.
The tie-break was invented by James Van Alen in 1965 to prevent excessively long sets.
Bjorn Borg won 11 Grand Slam titles before retiring at age 26, shocking the tennis world.
The 'serve and volley' style of play has become increasingly rare in modern tennis due to improved baseline play.
Rod Laver is the only player to complete the Grand Slam twice (1962 and 1969), winning all four majors in a calendar year.
The WTA (Women's Tennis Association) was founded in 1973 by Billie Jean King and 8 other players.
Andy Murray became the first British man to win Wimbledon in 77 years when he won in 2013.
A professional tennis player can burn between 400 and 600 calories per hour during a match.
The 'kick serve' produces heavy topspin that causes the ball to bounce high and to the side, making it difficult to return.
Naomi Osaka won 4 Grand Slam titles and sparked important conversations about mental health in professional sports.
The fastest women's serve was recorded at 136.7 mph by Georgina García Pérez in 2018.
The 'bagel' (6-0) and 'breadstick' (6-1) are tennis slang for lopsided set scores.
Althea Gibson was the first Black player to win a Grand Slam title, winning the French Open in 1956.
Tennis strings were traditionally made from natural gut (sheep or cow intestine) and are still preferred by many pros today.
The Indian Wells Masters and the Miami Open are known collectively as the 'Sunshine Double.'
Boris Becker won Wimbledon at age 17 in 1985, the youngest men's champion in the tournament's history at the time.
The 'tweener' — hitting the ball between the legs while facing away from the opponent — was popularized by Roger Federer.
Justine Henin's one-handed backhand is considered one of the best shots in the history of women's tennis.
The ATP Finals (formerly the Tour Finals) feature the top 8 players of the season in a round-robin format.
Kim Clijsters came out of retirement and won the 2009 US Open as an unseeded wild card — an unprecedented achievement.
Novak Djokovic is known for his incredible flexibility and his ability to return seemingly unreachable shots.
The 'deuce' in tennis comes from the French 'deux' (two), meaning two points are needed to win the game.
Ashleigh Barty retired as the world number 1 at age 25 in 2022, shocking the tennis world.
A typical Grand Slam tennis match uses about 42,000 tennis balls over the two-week tournament.
Stan Wawrinka won 3 Grand Slam titles, defeating the Big Three to win each one.
The 'volley' is a shot hit before the ball bounces, typically performed near the net.
Lindsay Davenport won 3 Grand Slam titles and held the world number 1 ranking for 98 weeks.
Tennis balls were originally white but were changed to optic yellow in 1972 for better visibility on television.
The 'drop shot' is a soft shot that barely clears the net and lands close to it, requiring the opponent to sprint forward.
Nick Kyrgios is known for his incredible talent, entertaining personality, and controversial on-court behavior.
Mats Wilander won 3 Grand Slam titles in 1988 and briefly held the world number 1 ranking.
The 'ace' — a serve that the opponent cannot touch — is one of the most decisive shots in tennis.
John Isner holds the record for most aces in professional tennis history.
The 'Challenge' system allows players to challenge line calls using electronic review, typically limited to 3 unsuccessful challenges per set.
Pat Rafter won back-to-back US Open titles in 1997 and 1998 with his serve-and-volley style.
The 'slice' imparts backspin on the ball, causing it to stay low after bouncing and float in the air longer.
Jannik Sinner won his first Grand Slam at the 2024 Australian Open, heralding a new era in men's tennis.
The 'love' in tennis scoring likely comes from the French word 'l'oeuf' (egg), representing zero.
The Centre Court at Wimbledon has a retractable roof installed in 2009, allowing play to continue during rain.
Gael Monfils is known as one of the most athletic and entertaining players on tour, famous for his diving shots.
The 'advantage' in tennis refers to the point scored after deuce — the player must win two consecutive points to win the game.
Jim Courier was the youngest world number 1 in men's tennis when he reached the top ranking at age 22 in 1992.
The WTA Finals feature the top 8 women's players and is one of the most prestigious events on the women's tour.
Martina Hingis became the youngest Grand Slam champion of the 20th century when she won the 1997 Australian Open at age 16.
The 'heavy ball' describes a shot with exceptional topspin that feels heavier than a typical shot when hitting it.
Goran Ivanisevic won Wimbledon in 2001 as a wild card, the only player to do so in the Open Era.
Tennis elbow (lateral epicondylitis) is a common overuse injury that affects both recreational and professional players.
The Fed Cup (now the Billie Jean King Cup) is the women's equivalent of the Davis Cup.
Caroline Wozniacki held the world number 1 ranking for 71 weeks and won the 2018 Australian Open.
The 'flat serve' has minimal spin and maximum speed, making it the hardest serve to return.
Daniil Medvedev defeated Novak Djokovic in the 2021 US Open final, preventing Djokovic from completing the calendar Grand Slam.
Virginia Wade was the last British woman to win Wimbledon in 1977 before it became a 40+ year drought.
The 'approach shot' is hit while moving toward the net, setting up a position for a volley.
Angelique Kerber won 3 Grand Slam titles and was the 2016 world number 1, known for her defensive counter-punching style.
The Hopman Cup was a mixed-gender international team event that ran from 1989 to 2019.
Dominic Thiem won the 2020 US Open, his only Grand Slam title, coming back from two sets down in the final.
A typical professional tennis match lasts about 1.5 to 2.5 hours, though Grand Slam matches can last 4+ hours.
The 'moon ball' — a high, loopy topspin shot — is used strategically to disrupt an opponent's rhythm.
Victoria Azarenka won 2 Australian Open titles and is known for her powerful baseline game and competitiveness.
The 'passing shot' is hit past a player who is at the net, often requiring precise placement.
Aryna Sabalenka won back-to-back Australian Open titles in 2023 and 2024 with her powerful game.
The 'net cord' is the wire cable at the top of the net — balls that hit it and go over are called 'net' or 'let' balls.
Lleyton Hewitt became the youngest year-end world number 1 in ATP history at age 20 in 2001.
The 'forehand' is typically the most powerful groundstroke and is hit on a player's dominant side.
The 'cross-court' shot travels diagonally across the court, while a 'down-the-line' shot travels parallel to the sideline.
Roger Federer's farewell match at the 2022 Laver Cup, partnering with rival Rafael Nadal, was one of the most emotional moments in sports.
The 'poaching' strategy in doubles involves the net player cutting across to intercept a return meant for their partner.
Simona Halep won the 2018 French Open and 2019 Wimbledon, reaching the world number 1 ranking.
Tennis rackets have evolved from wood to metal to graphite, with modern frames weighing about 10-12 ounces.
The 'second serve' is typically hit with more spin and less pace to ensure it goes in, as a missed second serve is a double fault.
Grigor Dimitrov, known as 'Baby Fed' for his similarity to Roger Federer's playing style, has reached the world top 3.
The Roland Garros complex in Paris hosts the French Open and is named after a World War I aviator.
A 'break of serve' occurs when the receiving player wins a game against the server, a crucial momentum shift.
Li Na was the first Asian-born player to win a Grand Slam singles title, winning the 2011 French Open.
The 'sunlight problem' at the US Open's Arthur Ashe Stadium has been alleviated by a retractable roof installed in 2016.
Alexander Zverev won the Olympic gold medal in 2021 and has reached multiple Grand Slam finals.
The 'split step' is a small hop made as the opponent strikes the ball, allowing the player to react quickly in any direction.
Hsieh Su-wei is famous for her unorthodox two-handed style on both forehand and backhand sides.
A 'walkover' (W/O) is recorded when a player advances because their opponent withdraws before the match.
Elena Rybakina won Wimbledon in 2022, becoming the first Kazakhstan-born player to win a Grand Slam.
Tennis was one of the original sports at the 1896 Olympics but was removed from 1924 to 1984 before returning in 1988.
The 'inside-out forehand' involves running around the backhand to hit a forehand, a strategy popularized by many modern players.
Gustavo Kuerten, 'Guga,' won three French Open titles and drew a heart on the clay after his victories.
The tension of tennis strings typically ranges from 40 to 70 pounds, with higher tension providing more control and lower tension more power.
Taylor Fritz has been the leading American men's player, aiming to end the country's Grand Slam drought.
The 'changeover' occurs every odd game, giving players 90 seconds to rest and switch sides of the court.
Doubles tennis requires different skills than singles, emphasizing net play, communication, and court positioning.
The 'no-ad' scoring system is used in some formats, where deuce is played as a sudden-death point.
Pete Sampras served an ace to win his 14th and final Grand Slam title at the 2002 US Open.
Karolina Muchova's incredible run to the 2023 French Open final showcased her creative shot-making and variety.
The 'Golden Set' — winning a set without losing a single point (24-0) — is one of tennis's rarest achievements.
Rafael Nadal's trademark 'fist pump' and intense on-court celebrations have become iconic in modern tennis.
The average rally in professional tennis lasts about 4-5 shots, though clay court rallies tend to be longer.
The 'one-handed backhand' is considered more aesthetically pleasing but less powerful than the two-handed version.
Tommy Haas played professional tennis for over 20 years and was known for his longevity and shot-making ability.
The 'warm-up' before a match typically lasts about 5 minutes and allows players to hit groundstrokes, volleys, and serves.
Frances Tiafoe's run to the 2022 US Open semifinals was one of the most exciting stories in American tennis history.
The speed gun measures serve velocity at the moment the ball leaves the racket, with readings displayed on the scoreboard.
The 'super tiebreak' (first to 10 points) is used in lieu of a third set in many doubles and mixed doubles matches.
Qinwen Zheng won the 2024 Olympic gold medal, becoming the first Chinese player to achieve the feat in singles.
Ball kids at Grand Slam events undergo rigorous training and are selected from hundreds of applicants.
The 'chip and charge' is a return strategy where the player hits a sliced return and immediately moves to the net.
The 'World Tennis Number' was introduced to provide a global rating system for recreational tennis players.
The original rules of lawn tennis were codified by Major Walter Clopton Wingfield in 1874 under the name 'Sphairistike.'
Before the Open Era began in 1968, Grand Slam tournaments were restricted to amateur players only.
The tiebreak was first used at a Grand Slam during the 1970 US Open after being introduced by James Van Alen.
Until 1975, seedings at Wimbledon were partly based on a player's perceived ability on grass rather than purely on rankings.
The service let rule — replaying a serve that clips the net — was experimentally abolished by some ATP events in 2020.
In 1970 the tiebreak was played at 8-all; it wasn't until 1979 that the current 6-all format became standard at most events.
Shot clocks were introduced on the ATP Tour in 2018 to enforce the 25-second time limit between points.
Coaching from the stands was banned in Grand Slams until 2022, when off-court coaching was officially trialed and then adopted.
The 'no-let' rule on serves was tested in select professional events and NextGen ATP Finals, eliminating serve lets.
Electronic line calling replaced all human line judges at the Australian Open starting in 2021.
The ATP introduced a best-of-three-set format for all events except Grand Slams starting in 2009.
Before 1971, women's Grand Slam finals were best of three sets; they have remained so since the Open Era began.
Until 2019, the final set at Wimbledon had no tiebreak; a final-set tiebreak at 12-all was introduced that year.
The French Open was the last Grand Slam to adopt a final-set tiebreak, introducing a super tiebreak at 6-all in the fifth set in 2022.
Self-officiating was standard in early tennis; the umpire's chair on a raised platform wasn't introduced until the late 1800s.
Real tennis — the medieval ancestor of modern lawn tennis — is still played on enclosed asymmetrical courts today.
Wooden tennis rackets dominated the sport until the late 1970s; the last Wimbledon singles title won with a wood racket was by Yannick Noah in 1983 at Roland Garros.
Howard Head introduced the first oversized aluminum racket, the Prince Classic, in 1976, revolutionizing the game for recreational players.
Graphite composite rackets appeared in the early 1980s and offered a combination of lightness and stiffness that wood and metal could not match.
Modern professional rackets typically have a head size of 95-100 square inches, compared to 65 square inches for classic wood frames.
Natural gut strings, made from cow intestine (serosa), are still used by many top professionals for their feel and power.
Polyester strings became popular in the late 1990s because they allow players to swing harder while maintaining control through heavy topspin.
Rafael Nadal's extreme RPM (revolutions per minute) on his forehand was enabled partly by the use of polyester strings.
Hybrid stringing — using one type of string for the mains and another for the crosses — became common in the 2000s for blending power and control.
Tennis balls are pressurized with approximately 14 psi of internal gas, which slowly leaks, causing balls to lose bounce.
Wilson has been the official ball supplier for the US Open since 1979 and for the Australian Open since 2006.
Dunlop supplies the balls for the Australian Open, while Babolat supplies for the French Open and Slazenger for Wimbledon.
The felt on a tennis ball is composed of a wool-nylon blend that affects aerodynamics, durability, and bounce.
Early tennis shoes were simple plimsolls; modern shoes feature herringbone patterns for clay and flat soles for hard courts.
Dampeners (or vibration absorbers) placed in the string bed reduce racket vibration but have no measurable effect on ball performance.
The Wilson T2000 steel racket, famously used by Jimmy Connors, was one of the first non-wood rackets to win Grand Slams.
Grass courts produce the fastest, lowest bounce of any surface, favoring serve-and-volley players with flat strokes.
Clay courts at Roland Garros use crushed brick (terre battue), which slows the ball, produces a high bounce, and favors baseline players.
The US Open switched from clay (Har-Tru) to hard courts (DecoTurf) in 1978, dramatically changing the style of play at Flushing Meadows.
The Australian Open was played on grass until 1988, when it moved to Rebound Ace hard courts; it switched to Plexicushion in 2008.
Indoor carpet courts were once common on the ATP Tour but were phased out entirely by 2009.
Green clay (Har-Tru) is popular in the United States and plays faster than the red clay used in Europe and South America.
Grass courts require between 6 months and a year to prepare and are made from perennial ryegrass cut to 8mm at Wimbledon.
The altitude of courts matters: at high-altitude venues like Bogotá, the thinner air makes the ball fly faster and bounce higher.
Blue clay was controversially introduced at the 2012 Madrid Open by Ion Tiriac but was abandoned after one year due to player complaints.
The lines on clay courts are made of metal tape embedded in the surface, whereas hard court and grass lines are painted.
Hard courts account for the majority of professional events because they offer a neutral, consistent bounce and lower maintenance.
The first Wimbledon Championship in 1877 featured only men's singles, with Spencer Gore winning the inaugural title.
The French Open (originally Championnats de France) was first held in 1891 but was only open to French nationals until 1925.
The US National Championship began in 1881 at the Newport Casino in Rhode Island before moving to Forest Hills and then Flushing Meadows.
The Australian Open was first held in 1905 and was played in different cities across Australia and New Zealand until settling permanently in Melbourne in 1972.
The Australian Open moved from January grass courts at Kooyong to the new Melbourne Park hard courts in 1988.
The US Open was the first Grand Slam to offer equal prize money to men and women, starting in 1973.
Wimbledon was the last Grand Slam to offer equal prize money, finally doing so in 2007.
The purple and green colors of Wimbledon were introduced in 1909 and have remained the tournament's signature ever since.
The Australian Open men's final is traditionally held on the last Sunday of January.
The French Open men's final is traditionally held on the second Sunday of June.
The Wimbledon men's final is traditionally held on the second Sunday of July.
The US Open men's final is traditionally held on the second Sunday of September.
The 'Challenge Round' system — where the reigning champion only had to play the final — was abolished at Wimbledon in 1922.
The Davis Cup was created by Dwight Davis in 1900 and is the oldest international team competition in tennis.
The Billie Jean King Cup (formerly the Fed Cup) began in 1963 as the Federation Cup and was renamed in 2020 in honor of Billie Jean King.
The ATP Finals (originally the Masters Grand Prix) have been held in cities including Tokyo, New York, Shanghai, London, and Turin.
The WTA Finals were first held in 1972 and have rotated among cities like Los Angeles, Singapore, Shenzhen, and Fort Worth.
The Monte-Carlo Masters has been held at the Monte-Carlo Country Club since 1897 and features stunning cliffside red clay courts.
The Italian Open (Internazionali d'Italia) was first held in 1930 and is one of the most prestigious clay-court events.
The Cincinnati Masters, officially the Western & Southern Open, has been held every year since 1899, making it one of the oldest events.
The Queen's Club Championships in London serve as Wimbledon's traditional warm-up grass-court event for men.
Roger Federer won 20 Grand Slam singles titles, held the world number 1 ranking for a total of 310 weeks, and was known for his graceful playing style.
Rafael Nadal's career win-loss record on clay courts is approximately 92%, the most dominant surface record in tennis history.
Novak Djokovic has won 7 Australian Open titles — more than any other player in history — earning the nickname 'King of Melbourne.'
Pete Sampras won 7 consecutive year-end number 1 rankings from 1993 to 1998, a record that still stands.
Andre Agassi famously went from flamboyant rebel to elder statesman, completing the career Grand Slam at the 1999 French Open.
Bjorn Borg's rivalry with John McEnroe in the late 1970s and early 1980s is considered one of the greatest in sports history.
Jimmy Connors won 109 career singles titles, more than any other male player in the Open Era.
Ivan Lendl was the first player to use rigorous physical fitness training as a cornerstone of his preparation, changing the sport.
Boris Becker won 6 Grand Slam titles and was known for his diving volleys and aggressive serve-and-volley style.
Stefan Edberg won 6 Grand Slam titles and is regarded as one of the finest serve-and-volley players ever.
Mats Wilander won 7 Grand Slam titles, including three in 1988, and was known for his versatility on all surfaces.
Guillermo Vilas won the 1977 French Open and US Open and was one of the first South American players to dominate the tour.
Ilie Nastase was the first ATP number 1 player in 1973 and was famous for both his talent and fiery temperament.
Manuel Orantes stunned the tennis world by beating Jimmy Connors in the 1975 US Open final after a remarkable comeback.
Ken Rosewall won 8 Grand Slam singles titles across the amateur and Open eras and was known for his precise backhand.
Pancho Gonzales was one of the most dominant players of the 1950s and 1960s, excelling as a professional in an era without Grand Slams open to pros.
Steffi Graf won 22 Grand Slam singles titles and spent a record 377 weeks at number 1, more than any player in history.
Martina Navratilova won 167 singles titles — the most in WTA history — and dominated Wimbledon with 9 singles titles.
Chris Evert won at least one Grand Slam title for 13 consecutive years (1974-1986) and had a career winning percentage of 90%.
Billie Jean King won 12 Grand Slam singles titles and was a pioneer for gender equality and LGBTQ+ rights in sports.
Margaret Court won 24 Grand Slam singles titles (11 in the Open Era), though many came against smaller fields before 1968.
Monica Seles won 9 Grand Slam titles before age 20 and was the youngest French Open champion at 16 in 1990.
Arantxa Sanchez Vicario won 4 Grand Slam singles titles and was known as the 'Barcelona Bumblebee' for her relentless court coverage.
Evonne Goolagong Cawley won 7 Grand Slam singles titles and was the first Indigenous Australian to become a sports star.
Virginia Wade won the Wimbledon singles title in 1977 during the tournament's centenary year in front of Queen Elizabeth II.
Tracy Austin became the youngest US Open women's champion at age 16 in 1979 before injuries cut short her career.
Gabriela Sabatini won the 1990 US Open and was one of the most popular players of her era.
Jannik Sinner reached world number 1 in June 2024 at age 22, becoming the first Italian man to hold the top ranking.
Carlos Alcaraz won the 2024 French Open and Wimbledon back to back, completing a Roland Garros-Wimbledon double at age 21.
Daniil Medvedev reached four Grand Slam finals and is known for his cerebral game and unconventional playing style.
Andrey Rublev has won multiple Masters 1000 titles and is recognized for his powerful forehand and emotional intensity.
Alexander Zverev has won the ATP Finals twice (2018, 2021) and the Olympic gold medal in Tokyo 2021.
Casper Ruud has reached three Grand Slam finals and is the highest-ranked Norwegian tennis player in history.
Stefanos Tsitsipas became the first Greek player to win an ATP Masters 1000 title and has reached the French Open final.
Holger Rune won the 2022 Paris Masters at age 19, defeating several top-10 players en route to the title.
Felix Auger-Aliassime has represented the new wave of Canadian tennis talent alongside Denis Shapovalov.
Hubert Hurkacz has won multiple ATP 1000 titles and is the top-ranked Polish men's player of the Open Era.
Ben Shelton's explosive serve and athleticism have made him one of the most exciting young Americans in tennis.
Iga Swiatek has won five Grand Slam titles, including four French Opens, dominating on clay with relentless topspin forehand.
Aryna Sabalenka won three consecutive Grand Slam titles (Australian Open 2023, 2024, and US Open 2024).
Coco Gauff won the 2023 US Open at age 19, becoming the youngest American Grand Slam champion since Serena Williams.
Elena Rybakina's powerful serve, averaging over 110 mph, makes her one of the most dangerous servers in women's tennis.
Jessica Pegula has established herself as a consistent top-10 player and a leading figure in American women's tennis.
Ons Jabeur became the first Arab and North African woman to reach a Grand Slam final at Wimbledon 2022.
Barbora Krejcikova won the 2021 French Open singles and doubles in the same year — a rare double achievement.
Marketa Vondrousova won Wimbledon 2023 as an unseeded player, the first unseeded women's champion in the Open Era.
Beatriz Haddad Maia has become the highest-ranked Brazilian player in WTA history.
Jack Draper has emerged as Britain's top men's prospect, combining a big left-handed serve with powerful groundstrokes.
Centre Court at Wimbledon seats approximately 15,000 spectators and has hosted every Wimbledon singles final since 1922.
Arthur Ashe Stadium at the USTA Billie Jean King National Tennis Center is the largest tennis-specific stadium in the world, seating 23,771.
Court Philippe Chatrier at Roland Garros seats 15,225 spectators and received a retractable roof in 2020.
Rod Laver Arena in Melbourne seats 14,820 people and has featured a retractable roof since 1988.
Louis Armstrong Stadium at the US Open was rebuilt in 2018 with a retractable roof and seats 14,053.
The O2 Arena in London hosted the ATP Finals from 2009 to 2020 before the event moved to Turin's Pala Alpitour.
The Indian Wells Tennis Garden features a 16,100-seat main stadium in the California desert.
Court Suzanne Lenglen at Roland Garros is the second-largest court and received a retractable roof in 2024.
The All England Lawn Tennis Club (AELTC) purchased the adjacent Wimbledon Park Golf Club to expand its grounds.
Margaret Court Arena in Melbourne is the third show court at the Australian Open and received a retractable roof in 2015.
The 1980 Wimbledon final between Bjorn Borg and John McEnroe featured a legendary fourth-set tiebreak that McEnroe won 18-16 before Borg took the fifth set.
The 2008 Wimbledon final between Rafael Nadal and Roger Federer lasted nearly five hours, delayed by rain, and ended in near-darkness with Nadal winning.
The 2009 Australian Open final between Rafael Nadal and Roger Federer ended with Federer in tears after losing a five-set classic.
The 2012 Australian Open final between Novak Djokovic and Rafael Nadal lasted 5 hours 53 minutes — the longest Grand Slam final in history.
The 2001 Wimbledon final saw wildcard Goran Ivanisevic defeat Pat Rafter in a dramatic five-set rain-delayed Monday final.
The Battle of the Sexes on September 20, 1973, at the Houston Astrodome drew 30,472 fans — the largest live tennis audience at the time.
The 2019 Wimbledon final between Novak Djokovic and Roger Federer was the longest Wimbledon singles final in history at 4 hours 57 minutes.
The 2005 Australian Open final between Marat Safin and Lleyton Hewitt was a five-set epic that established Safin's legacy as a big-occasion player.
The 1988 French Open semifinal between Mats Wilander and Guillermo Perez Roldan showcased grueling baseline rallies over nearly five hours.
The 2017 Australian Open final between Roger Federer and Rafael Nadal was celebrated as a fairy-tale comeback for both aging rivals.
The 2004 Wimbledon final saw 17-year-old Maria Sharapova stun defending champion Serena Williams in straight sets.
The 1984 French Open final saw Ivan Lendl overcome John McEnroe from two sets down — one of the greatest comebacks in Grand Slam history.
Novak Djokovic won his 24th Grand Slam singles title at the 2023 US Open, tying Margaret Court's all-time record.
Rafael Nadal won his first French Open on June 5, 2005, at age 19, beginning an unprecedented dynasty on clay.
Roger Federer won his first Wimbledon title on July 6, 2003, defeating Mark Philippoussis in straight sets.
Serena Williams won her first Grand Slam at the 1999 US Open at age 17.
Arthur Ashe won Wimbledon on July 5, 1975, stunning heavily favored Jimmy Connors with intelligent, tactical tennis.
Monica Seles was stabbed on court at the Hamburg Open on April 30, 1993, altering the course of women's tennis history.
Andy Murray won his first Wimbledon on July 7, 2013, ending a 77-year drought for British men in the tournament.
Carlos Alcaraz won the 2023 Wimbledon title on July 16, defeating Novak Djokovic in a five-set final.
The longest match in tennis history — Isner vs. Mahut — ended on June 24, 2010, with a final-set score of 70-68.
Steffi Graf completed the Golden Slam on October 1, 1988, by winning the Olympic gold medal in Seoul.
Tennis returned to the Olympic program at the 1988 Seoul Games after a 64-year absence.
The Open Era began on April 22, 1968, when the British Hard Court Championships became the first Open tournament.
Billie Jean King and eight other players signed one-dollar contracts on September 23, 1970, to form the original Virginia Slims tour, precursor to the WTA.
The 'advantage set' format — where a two-game lead is needed — was the standard for all Grand Slam final sets until 2019.
Foot-fault calls require the server's feet to remain behind the baseline without touching or crossing it during the service motion.
The first automated line-calling system was 'Cyclops,' which detected service faults using infrared beams from the 1980s to 2007.
Medical timeouts allow players up to three minutes for on-court treatment and are limited in number per match.
Continuous play rules prohibit coaching and excessive delays between points, with time violations resulting in warnings and point penalties.
The 'Grand Slam' term was first used in tennis in 1933 by New York Times journalist John Kieran, borrowing it from bridge.
Wheelchair tennis was introduced as a Paralympic sport at the 1992 Barcelona Games and uses a two-bounce rule.
The ITF (International Tennis Federation) governs the rules of tennis globally and oversees the Davis Cup and Billie Jean King Cup.
The Babolat Pure Aero, used by Rafael Nadal, is designed to maximize topspin with an aerodynamic beam shape.
Wilson's Pro Staff RF97, co-designed with Roger Federer, features a 97-square-inch head and weighs 340 grams unstrung.
HEAD's Gravity racket line was developed with Alexander Zverev and features an asymmetric head shape for versatile play.
String gauges range from 15 (thickest) to 19 (thinnest); thinner strings offer more spin potential but break faster.
Tennis ball manufacturers produce approximately 300 million balls annually; most are not recyclable and end up in landfills.
The International Tennis Federation specifies that balls must bounce between 53 and 58 inches when dropped from 100 inches onto concrete.
Pressureless tennis balls maintain their bounce longer than pressurized balls and are commonly used for practice.
Wearable technology like the Babolat Play racket and Zepp sensor allows players to track swing speed, spin, and impact location.
Jan-Michael Gambill, Todd Martin, and MaliVai Washington carried American men's tennis during the late 1990s between Agassi/Sampras eras.
Yevgeny Kafelnikov won the 1996 French Open and 1999 Australian Open and was the first Russian man to hold the world number 1 ranking.
Gustavo Kuerten drew a heart on the clay at Roland Garros after winning his third French Open title in 2001.
Patrick Rafter's back-to-back US Open titles in 1997-98 were built on exceptional serve-and-volley play and athleticism.
Lleyton Hewitt's fighting spirit and athleticism made him a two-time Grand Slam champion and Davis Cup hero for Australia.
Michael Chang became the youngest male Grand Slam champion when he won the 1989 French Open at age 17.
Thomas Muster won the 1995 French Open after recovering from a serious knee injury sustained when he was hit by a car.
Amelie Mauresmo won the 2006 Australian Open and Wimbledon, becoming the first openly gay Grand Slam champion.
Mary Pierce won the 2000 French Open and was known for her powerful groundstrokes and dual French-American identity.
Jana Novotna famously wept on the Duchess of Kent's shoulder after losing the 1993 Wimbledon final before winning the title in 1998.
Conchita Martinez won Wimbledon in 1994 — the only Spanish woman to win the title — and later coached Garbine Muguruza to Grand Slam titles.
Hana Mandlikova won 4 Grand Slam titles in the 1980s, holding her own against Navratilova and Evert.
Juan Martin del Potro's thunderous forehand helped him defeat Roger Federer in the 2009 US Open final.
David Ferrer reached the 2013 French Open final and was known as one of the hardest workers in tennis history.
Flavia Pennetta won the 2015 US Open and announced her retirement during the trophy ceremony.
Jelena Ostapenko shocked the world by winning the 2017 French Open as an unseeded player, the first Latvian Grand Slam champion.
Bianca Andreescu won the 2019 US Open at age 19, becoming the first Canadian to win a Grand Slam singles title.
Emma Raducanu won the 2021 US Open as a qualifier without dropping a set, a feat unprecedented in the Open Era.
Andy Roddick's 155 mph serve at the 2004 Davis Cup was the fastest officially recorded serve at the time.
Robin Soderling was the first player to defeat Rafael Nadal at the French Open, accomplishing the feat in 2009.
Tommy Paul has risen to become one of the top American men's players with his all-court game and improved consistency.
Alex de Minaur is the top-ranked Australian men's player and is known for his exceptional speed and defensive skills.
Lorenzo Musetti represents Italy's deep pool of talent and is known for his elegant single-handed backhand.
Karen Khachanov reached the 2023 Australian Open semifinal and won the 2018 Paris Masters.
Grigor Dimitrov has experienced a career resurgence, reaching the late rounds of Grand Slams with his versatile one-handed backhand game.
Sebastian Korda, son of 1998 Australian Open champion Petr Korda, has built a rising career on the ATP Tour.
Zheng Qinwen won the 2024 Olympic women's singles gold medal and has become China's top singles player.
Jasmine Paolini reached the finals of both the French Open and Wimbledon in 2024, a breakthrough for Italian women's tennis.
Mirra Andreeva entered the top 20 as a teenager and is considered one of the brightest WTA prospects.
Donna Vekic reached the 2024 US Open semifinal and has been Croatia's most successful women's player.
The grass at Wimbledon is a perennial ryegrass blend cut to exactly 8 millimeters during the Championships.
Clay courts require daily watering and rolling to maintain proper playing conditions.
The US Open is the only Grand Slam played under floodlights during evening sessions, a tradition since the move to Flushing Meadows in 1978.
Roland Garros introduced night sessions under floodlights for the first time at the 2021 tournament.
The surface speed index (SPI) developed by the ITF rates courts from 'slow' (clay) through 'medium' (hard) to 'fast' (grass).
Wimbledon's No. 1 Court was rebuilt in 2019 with a retractable roof and seats 12,345 spectators.
The Madrid Open is played at altitude (650m above sea level), making the ball fly faster and affecting spin.
The Bryan brothers (Bob and Mike) won 16 Grand Slam men's doubles titles together, the most successful team in Open Era history.
Martina Navratilova and Pam Shriver won 109 consecutive doubles matches from 1983 to 1985, including all four Grand Slams in 1984.
Mixed doubles was featured at all four Grand Slams and was an Olympic event in 2012 and 2024.
Doubles uses the wider tramlines (alleys), giving each team 36 feet of court width compared to 27 feet in singles.
The 'I-formation' in doubles positions both players near the center of the baseline and net, designed to confuse the returner.
The phrase 'tennis bracelet' originated in 1987 when Chris Evert's diamond bracelet fell off during a US Open match and play was stopped to find it.
Strawberries and cream have been served at Wimbledon since 1877, with approximately 28,000 kg consumed each tournament.
The queue at Wimbledon is an iconic British tradition where fans camp overnight for unreserved ground passes.
Pimm's Cup is the signature cocktail served at Wimbledon, with approximately 320,000 glasses consumed each tournament.
Rafael Nadal's superstitions include arranging water bottles in a precise pattern and always stepping over the baseline with his right foot.
The Tennis Hall of Fame is located in Newport, Rhode Island, at the International Tennis Club, which hosted early US Championships.
Tennis has been depicted in films like 'Wimbledon' (2004), 'Borg vs McEnroe' (2017), and 'King Richard' (2021).
The ATP Race to Turin determines the eight qualifiers for the year-end ATP Finals based on calendar-year results.
Prize money at the 2024 US Open reached over $65 million, the highest total purse of any Grand Slam.
The four Grand Slams collectively generate over $1 billion in annual revenue from broadcasting, sponsorship, and ticketing.
Tennis is played in over 200 countries and the ITF has 213 member nations, making it one of the most global sports.
Patrick Mouratoglou, coach of Serena Williams from 2012 onward, also founded a tennis academy in France that trains elite juniors.
Toni Nadal coached his nephew Rafael from childhood to 2017, instilling the mental toughness that defined Rafa's career.
Darren Cahill, former coach of Andre Agassi and Simona Halep, is known for his analytical approach and on-court composure.
The 'first-strike tennis' philosophy emphasizes ending points early through aggressive serving and return games.
Modern analytics track metrics like forehand winners per match, unforced errors, net approaches, and second-serve win percentage.
Professional tennis players typically run 2 to 3 miles per match, with clay-court matches involving the most running.
Novak Djokovic credits his gluten-free diet, adopted in 2010, with transforming his stamina and on-court performance.
Heat-related illness is a concern at the Australian Open, which introduced an Extreme Heat Policy when temperatures exceed 40°C (104°F).
Tennis requires explosive sprinting, lateral movement, and the ability to change direction up to 500 times per match.
Wrist injuries have become more common in the modern game due to the extreme spin generated by polyester strings.
Novak Djokovic holds the record for the most weeks at ATP number 1, surpassing 420 weeks.
Roger Federer reached 30 consecutive Grand Slam semifinal appearances from 2004 to 2011, a record of sustained excellence.
Serena Williams won Grand Slam titles across four decades: the 1990s, 2000s, 2010s, and 2017.
Margaret Court's 64 Grand Slam titles (including singles, doubles, and mixed) is the all-time record.
Rafael Nadal won 14 French Open titles between 2005 and 2022, losing only 4 matches at Roland Garros in his career.
Martina Navratilova won her last Grand Slam mixed doubles title at the 2006 US Open at age 49.
Ken Rosewall holds the record as the oldest Grand Slam singles champion in the Open Era, winning the 1972 Australian Open at age 37.
Iga Swiatek won 37 consecutive matches in 2022, the longest winning streak in women's tennis since Venus Williams in 2000.
John Isner has hit over 14,000 career aces, the most in ATP history.
Chris Evert's career winning percentage of 89.97% is the highest in WTA history.
Tennis was originally played with bare hands before rackets were introduced in the 16th century.
The word 'tennis' likely derives from the French tenez, meaning 'take heed' or 'receive,' called out before serving.
Yellow tennis balls were first used at Wimbledon in 1986 — 14 years after they were introduced in professional tennis.
The 1877 Wimbledon final was watched by about 200 spectators; today Centre Court holds nearly 15,000.
Suzanne Lenglen, the first female tennis celebrity, was so dominant in the 1920s she lost only one match in seven years.
The Davis Cup has been contested by over 140 nations, making it the largest annual international team competition in any sport.
The fastest women's serve recorded at a Grand Slam was 131 mph by Venus Williams at the 2007 US Open.
During World War II, Wimbledon's grounds were used by military as a farmyard, fire station, and decontamination base.
The ATP Tour visits over 30 countries annually, with tournaments spanning every continent except Antarctica.
Night sessions were introduced at the US Open in 1975 and have become some of the most electric atmospheres in tennis.
Nick Bollettieri's IMG Academy in Bradenton, Florida, trained champions including Agassi, Courier, Seles, Sharapova, and the Williams sisters.
The 'Super Saturday' at the 1984 US Open featured three classic matches including McEnroe-Connors and Lendl-Cash back to back.
Sampras–Agassi is the most-played rivalry in ATP Finals history; they met 34 times across their careers.
Novak Djokovic won the 2023 French Open to claim his 23rd Grand Slam title, matching Serena Williams' Open Era record.
Roscoe Tanner's left-handed serve was once called the fastest in the world and helped him reach the 1979 Wimbledon final.
The WTA introduced on-court coaching in 2012, allowing coaches to come to the court once per set during tour-level events.
John Newcombe won 7 Grand Slam singles titles and was one of the dominant players of the late 1960s and early 1970s.
Court Philippe Chatrier at Roland Garros is named after the former French Tennis Federation president who championed tennis' return to the Olympics.
The Hopman Cup was replaced by the ATP Cup in 2020, a change that disappointed many players and fans.
Yannick Noah is the last Frenchman to win the French Open, triumphing in 1983 — over 40 years ago.
Garbine Muguruza won the 2016 French Open and 2017 Wimbledon before retiring at age 30 in 2023.
Sloane Stephens won the 2017 US Open after coming back from a foot injury that kept her out for 11 months.
The United States has won the Davis Cup a record 32 times, followed by Australia with 28 wins.
The Next Gen ATP Finals, featuring players 21 and under, use experimental rules like shorter sets and no-ad scoring.
Henri Cochet, Jean Borotra, Jacques Brugnon, and René Lacoste — the 'Four Musketeers' — dominated tennis in the 1920s and 1930s.
Denis Shapovalov's one-handed backhand and lefty serve have made him one of the most exciting young players in men's tennis.
Petra Kvitova won Wimbledon in 2011 and 2014 with her powerful left-handed game and later overcame a knife attack to return to the top.
The longest women's match in Grand Slam history was the 2011 Australian Open quarterfinal between Francesca Schiavone and Svetlana Kuznetsova, lasting 4 hours 44 minutes.
Anna Kournikova never won a WTA singles title but was one of the most famous tennis players in the world due to her celebrity profile.
Richard Gasquet has been described as having the best one-handed backhand in modern tennis, often compared to a work of art.
The clay at Roland Garros is 2-3 millimeters thick, laid over a foundation of limestone and clinker.
Daria Kasatkina reached the French Open semifinals in 2022 and is known for her creative shot-making and drop shots.
Stan Smith, the 1972 Wimbledon champion, may be more famous today for the Adidas sneaker that bears his name.
Jo-Wilfried Tsonga reached the 2008 Australian Open final and was one of the most athletic and charismatic players of his generation.
Max Purcell and Matt Ebden won the 2024 Australian Open men's doubles title, continuing Australia's rich doubles tradition.
Andre Agassi founded the Andre Agassi College Preparatory Academy in Las Vegas, providing free education to at-risk children.
The 'golden swing' refers to hitting a forehand with the non-dominant hand — an emergency shot used in desperate defensive situations.
Rafael Nadal's uncle Miguel Angel Nadal was a professional soccer player for FC Barcelona and Spain's national team.
The 2023 WTA Finals were held in Cancun, Mexico, marking the first time the event took place in Latin America.
Caroline Garcia won the 2022 WTA Finals, capping off a resurgent second half of the season.
